---
title: "Local Networking in Detail"
---

## Requirements and protocol choice

The network feature has two use cases:

1. a read-only live caption viewer;
2. anonymous text question submission.

Both must work without installing an app. The server therefore uses HTTP
semantics understood by every modern browser.

The caption stream uses Server-Sent Events (SSE), not WebSocket:

- traffic is one-way from Mac to viewer;
- `EventSource` reconnects automatically;
- messages remain ordinary UTF-8 text;
- there is no client command channel to secure;
- implementation is smaller.

Questions use bounded HTTP POST requests because they are discrete mutations.

## Network.framework listener

`NWParameters.tcp` creates a TCP listener. The application sets:

```swift
parameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wifi
parameters.includePeerToPeer = true
parameters.allowLocalEndpointReuse = true
```

The listener is therefore constrained to Wi-Fi paths, supports Apple
peer-to-peer-capable paths where the system permits, and can reclaim the local
port after restart.

It binds port 8765 and advertises Bonjour service type:

```text
_classcaptions._tcp
```

Bonjour is DNS Service Discovery over multicast DNS. The browser flow currently
uses the IPv4 URL printed in the QR code; service advertisement prepares for
native discovery and diagnostics.

All mutable server state lives on one serial `DispatchQueue`. Network.framework
callbacks are scheduled on that same queue, so dictionaries, connection sets,
tokens, and rate windows do not require independent locks.

## Finding the advertised address

After the listener reaches `.ready`, the server enumerates interfaces using
`getifaddrs`, ignores loopback and down interfaces, and selects an IPv4 address.
It prefers `en0`, the conventional Wi-Fi interface on many Macs, then uses the
first viable fallback.

The resulting URL resembles:

```text
http://192.168.1.42:8765/?token=<64 hexadecimal characters>
```

The IP is private-address routing information, not a globally reachable host.
Network isolation, client isolation, VPN routing, and firewalls can still
prevent peer access.

## Connection lifecycle

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant Browser
    participant Listener as NWListener
    participant Conn as NWConnection
    participant Parser
    participant App

    Browser->>Listener: TCP SYN / HTTP request
    Listener->>Conn: new connection
    Conn->>Parser: receive up to 2048-byte chunks
    Parser->>Parser: accumulate until CRLF CRLF
    Parser->>Parser: validate total and Content-Length
    Parser->>App: route authenticated request
    App-->>Conn: response or persistent SSE
```

The server allows at most 64 simultaneous accepted TCP connections. Each is
identified by `ObjectIdentifier(connection)` until cancellation, failure, or
response completion.

## Minimal HTTP parser

This is intentionally not a general web server. It supports only the requests
the generated pages issue.

### Framing

Data is accumulated until the byte sequence `\r\n\r\n` marks the end of headers.
Total request size is capped at 8 KiB. Body content is capped at 2 KiB.

The parser reads `Content-Length`, calculates:

```text
expected bytes = end-of-header offset + content length
```

and continues receiving until that count is available. Connections are closed
after ordinary responses, so request pipelining and keep-alive parsing are not
implemented.

### Request line

The first line is split into exactly:

```text
METHOD request-target HTTP-version
```

`URLComponents` parses the request target into path and query items. Header
names are normalized to lowercase.

### Route table

| Method | Path | Credential | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| GET | `/` | viewer token | caption HTML |
| GET | `/events` | viewer token | SSE caption stream |
| GET | `/student` | student token | question HTML |
| POST | `/student-ticket` | student token | issue browser ticket |
| POST | `/questions` | source-bound ticket | submit one question |

Everything else returns the same `404 Not Found`, including protected routes
with a wrong token. This avoids an authentication oracle that reveals whether a
route exists.

## Pairing tokens

`SecRandomCopyBytes` produces 32 random bytes. Each byte is rendered as two
lowercase hexadecimal characters, yielding 64 characters and 256 bits of
entropy.

Comparison first requires equal byte length, then XORs every pair into one
accumulator:

```swift
var difference: UInt8 = 0
for index in left.indices {
    difference |= left[index] ^ right[index]
}
return difference == 0
```

For equal-length inputs this avoids returning at the first differing byte.

Viewer and student tokens are independent. Stopping sharing erases both.
Pausing questions erases only the student token and all tickets.

## Caption page and SSE

The HTML is generated inside the executable. It contains no external scripts,
fonts, images, analytics, or package dependencies.

JavaScript creates:

```javascript
const source = new EventSource(
  "/events?token=" + encodeURIComponent(token)
);
```

The server responds:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate
Connection: keep-alive

retry: 1000
```

Caption messages use:

```text
event: captions
data: {"finalized":[...],"provisional":"...","sessionActive":true,"revision":42}

```

The blank line ends one SSE event. JSON encoding escapes embedded newlines and
other string characters. The browser parses `event.data`, replaces its caption
DOM children, and creates text nodes through `textContent`; caption text is
never assigned to `innerHTML`.

A comment heartbeat:

```text
: heartbeat

```

is sent every ten seconds. SSE ignores comment lines, but traffic keeps NAT,
Wi-Fi, and browser connection state active.

Only one caption SSE connection is accepted. A second receives `409 Conflict`.
Ordinary page requests and student submissions are separate connections.

## Snapshot model

The server stores the latest `RemoteCaptionSnapshot`. When a viewer connects,
that snapshot is sent immediately, so the page does not wait for the next spoken
token.

Snapshots contain only:

- three visible finalized strings;
- current provisional string;
- session-active Boolean;
- monotonic revision.

They contain no command, endpoint, model configuration, question text, file
path, or microphone control capability.

## Student ticket protocol

The student QR token grants access to the page and ticket endpoint. It is not
used directly for every question.

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Student browser
    participant Server
    participant Q as Professor queue

    S->>Server: GET /student?token=studentToken
    Server-->>S: HTML + embedded token
    S->>Server: POST /student-ticket?token=studentToken
    Server-->>S: random ticket, maxCharacters=500
    S->>Server: POST /questions?ticket=ticket + JSON
    Server->>Server: authenticate source, normalize, rate-limit
    Server->>Q: SubmittedClassroomQuestion
    Server-->>S: 202 Accepted
```

The ticket is another 256-bit token stored only in memory. It records:

- source ID derived from remote endpoint;
- expiration time four hours later;
- submission timestamps.

Binding to source reduces the usefulness of a copied ticket. It is an abuse
control, not user identity: NAT may cause many students to share one source
address, and mobile network changes may invalidate a ticket.

## Question validation and limits

Text is decoded from a JSON object with exactly the needed `text` member.
Whitespace runs collapse to one space. Empty text, control characters, and text
over 500 Swift characters are rejected.

Limits are:

| Limit | Value |
| --- | ---: |
| minimum interval per ticket | 10 seconds |
| per source | 8 per 5 minutes |
| classroom-wide | 30 per minute |
| active tickets | 256 |
| accepted questions during enabled period | 100 |
| Mac pending queue | 100 |

The source address is not shown in the UI or archived.

## HTTP response hardening

Ordinary responses include:

- `Cache-Control: no-store`;
- `Connection: close`;
- `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`;
- `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`;
- `X-Frame-Options: DENY`;
- Content Security Policy restricting content to the generated page itself.

The CSP permits inline script and style because both are compiled into the
single page. It denies objects, frames, external images, and base-URL changes.

## What HTTP does not provide

The token authenticates possession of the QR link. Plain HTTP does not encrypt:

- captions;
- question text;
- tokens in request targets;
- source and destination addresses.

A hostile network operator can observe or modify traffic. The feature is
appropriate for a trusted Wi-Fi network or personal hotspot, not an untrusted
public network. HTTPS would require certificate provisioning and browser trust;
a future native client can instead use an authenticated encrypted peer channel.
